serving.natural_language_portion: One five-spice chicken roll weighing approximately 80 g, typically served as a side dish or snack alongside rice or noodles.

nutrient_source.title: Ngoh Hiang / Five-Spice Meat Roll — HPB Singapore Foodo / USDA FoodData Central analogous composition for seasoned minced chicken roll, deep-fried
nutrient_source.organization: Health Promotion Board Singapore (HPB) / USDA FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: HPB Singapore Foodo lists ngoh hiang (five-spice meat roll) as a comparable preparation. Values are adapted from HPB ngoh hiang entry and cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central data for seasoned minced chicken products. Five-spice chicken roll is a chicken-only variant of the classic pork-and-prawn ngoh hiang; macros adjusted to reflect chicken-dominant composition. Confidence is medium due to adaptation.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of mixed protein-fat-carbohydrate savoury snack foods — analogous estimate based on deep-fried seasoned minced meat roll with beancurd skin wrapper
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement found for five-spice chicken roll. GI estimated at 40 (low) based on analogous deep-fried savoury meat rolls with beancurd skin wrappers listed in the University of Sydney GI Database. The high protein and fat content, combined with the low carbohydrate fraction from beancurd skin and water chestnuts, supports a low GI classification. GL per serving = (9.4 g carbohydrate per 100g × 80g serving / 100) × 40 / 100 = approximately 3.0.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content (480 mg per 100 g)
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit consumption of five-spice chicken roll due to its high sodium content of approximately 480 mg per 100 g; a single 80 g serving contributes around 384 mg sodium, which is a significant proportion of the recommended daily limit of 2000 mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index (~40) and moderate carbohydrate content
type2_diabetes.guidance: Five-spice chicken roll has a low estimated glycemic index of approximately 40 and a glycemic load of about 3 per serving, making it a relatively suitable protein-rich option for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes when consumed in moderation and as part of a balanced meal.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Moderate saturated fat and high sodium from deep-frying and seasoning
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should consume five-spice chicken roll in moderation due to its saturated fat content (3.2 g per 100 g) and high sodium (480 mg per 100 g) from deep-frying and soy-based seasoning, both of which may adversely affect blood pressure and lipid profiles.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Five-Spice Chicken Roll (五香鸡卷, Wu Xiang Ji Juan) is a Singaporean Chinese savoury snack and side dish made from seasoned minced chicken wrapped in dried tofu skin (beancurd skin / fu pei) and deep-fried until golden and crispy. It is a chicken-only variant of the classic Singaporean-Hokkien dish ngoh hiang (五香), which traditionally uses a mixture of pork, prawns, and water chestnuts. The filling is seasoned with five-spice powder (a blend of star anise, cloves, Chinese cinnamon, Sichuan pepper, and fennel seeds), soy sauce, sesame oil, egg, and finely chopped water chestnuts and spring onions, giving it a fragrant, savoury, and slightly sweet flavour profile with a crispy exterior and juicy interior. The dish is commonly served as a hawker centre snack, a side dish alongside rice or noodles, or as part of a mixed plate. Nutritionally, it provides approximately 218 kcal per 100 g, with 14.2 g protein, 13.8 g fat, and 9.4 g carbohydrate. The sodium content is relatively high at 480 mg per 100 g due to soy sauce and seasoning, making portion control important for individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease. Its low glycemic index (~40) makes it a reasonable protein source for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes when consumed in moderation.

confidence_reason: No exact HPB Singapore Foodo entry for five-spice chicken roll as a standalone chicken-only product was found. Nutrient values are adapted from HPB ngoh hiang (five-spice meat roll) data and cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central for seasoned minced chicken preparations. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database based on comparable deep-fried savoury meat rolls. Confidence is medium due to adaptation rather than exact match.
notes: Vitamin D (vitamin_d_mcg) is -1 as it is not reported in the HPB or USDA source data for this food category. Vitamin E (vitamin_e_mg) is -1 as it is not reported in the primary source data. Selenium (selenium_mcg) is -1 as it is not reported in the primary source data. Trans fat is reported as 0.1 g per 100 g reflecting trace amounts from deep-frying; this is a plausible trace value consistent with deep-fried poultry products. GI of 40 is an analogous estimate; direct measurement for this specific product is not available in the University of Sydney GI Database. GL per serving calculated as: (9.4 × 80 / 100) × (40 / 100) = 3.01, rounded to 3.0. Halal status is assumed based on chicken-only composition and typical hawker preparation; individual restaurant certification should be verified. The cuisine_tags field in the input JSON incorrectly listed the restaurant name as the cuisine tag; this has been corrected to Singaporean Chinese.
